To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel collaborative learning framework for TKGR (dubbed CID-TKG) that integrates evolutionary dynamics and historical invariance semantics as an effective inductive bias for reasoning. Specifically, CID-TKG constructs a historical invariance graph to capture long-term structural regularities and an evolutionary dynamics graph to model short-term temporal transitions. Dedicated encoders are then employed to learn representations from each structure. To alleviate semantic discrepancies across the two structures, we decompose relations into view-specific representations and align view-specific query representations via a contrastive objective, which promotes cross-view consistency while suppressing view-specific noise. Extensive experiments verify that our CID-TKG achieves state-of-the-art performance under extrapolation settings.

AI.tldr: CID-TKG: A collaborative learning framework for temporal knowledge graph reasoning that integrates evolutionary dynamics and historical invariance semantics to overcome limitations of existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing TKG reasoning approaches suffer from inherent limitations due to inductive biases - they rely on time-invariant or weakly time-dependent structures and overlook evolutionary dynamics, which hinders accurate future fact inference.
AI.method: Proposes CID-TKG framework that constructs: 1) historical invariance graph to capture long-term structural regularities, 2) evolutionary dynamics graph to model short-term temporal transitions. Uses dedicated encoders for each structure, decomposes relations into view-specific representations, and aligns query representations via contrastive objective to alleviate semantic discrepancies.
AI.result: Extensive experiments verify that CID-TKG achieves state-of-the-art performance under extrapolation settings for temporal knowledge graph reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The collaborative learning framework integrating evolutionary dynamics and historical invariance semantics provides an effective inductive bias for TKG reasoning, overcoming limitations of existing approaches and achieving superior performance.

title: AffordGen: Generating Diverse Demonstrations for Generalizable Object Manipulation with Afford Correspondence
summary: Despite the recent success of modern imitation learning methods in robot manipulation, their performance is often constrained by geometric variations due to limited data diversity. Leveraging powerful 3D generative models and vision foundation models (VFMs), the proposed AffordGen framework overcomes this limitation by utilizing the semantic correspondence of meaningful keypoints across large-scale 3D meshes to generate new robot manipulation trajectories. This large-scale, affordance-aware dataset is then used to train a robust, closed-loop visuomotor policy, combining the semantic generalizability of affordances with the reactive robustness of end-to-end learning. Experiments in simulation and the real world show that policies trained with AffordGen achieve high success rates and enable zero-shot generalization to truly unseen objects, significantly improving data efficiency in robot learning.

AI.tldr: AffordGen uses 3D generative models and vision foundation models to create affordance-aware datasets for training robust robot manipulation policies that generalize to unseen objects.
AI.motivation: Current imitation learning methods for robot manipulation are constrained by limited data diversity and poor generalization to geometric variations in objects.
AI.method: Leverages 3D generative models and vision foundation models to extract semantic keypoint correspondences across large-scale 3D meshes, generating new manipulation trajectories to create an affordance-aware dataset for training closed-loop visuomotor policies.
AI.result: Policies trained with AffordGen achieve high success rates in simulation and real-world experiments, enabling zero-shot generalization to truly unseen objects and significantly improving data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The AffordGen framework successfully combines semantic affordance generalizability with reactive robustness of end-to-end learning to overcome data diversity limitations in robot manipulation.

title: Prior-Guided Movable Antenna Control for Agile Multi-Path Sensing (extended version)
summary: Multi-path sensing, which aims to extract the geometric attributes of multiple propagation paths, is expected to be a key functionality of 6G. A movable antenna (MA) can enable this functionality by creating a synthetic aperture through sequential mechanical motion. However, existing MA-based sensing methods typically rely on exhaustive scanning over the entire movable plate, resulting in significant control overhead and sensing latency, which limits their practicality for agile sensing. To address this challenge, this paper develops a prior-guided agile multi-path sensing framework that leverages weak prior angle-of-arrival (AoA) statistics as side information. The proposed framework comprises two steps. First, the movable plate's three-dimensional orientation is optimized only once to maximize path visibility while preserving path discriminability, both induced from Fisher information analysis. Second, only two predetermined linear MA scans are made on the tilted plate to estimate the elevation and azimuth AoAs from the resulting sequence of received signals. By incorporating the prior AoA statistics, a maximum a posteriori (MAP)-based AoA estimation algorithm is developed. With only one orientation control and two linear scans, the proposed framework enables agile multi-path sensing with significantly reduced control overhead and latency, while achieving AoA estimation accuracy approaching that of the single-path benchmark.

AI.tldr: A prior-guided agile multi-path sensing framework using movable antennas with optimized 3D orientation and two linear scans to reduce control overhead while maintaining AoA estimation accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing MA-based sensing methods require exhaustive scanning over entire movable plates, causing significant control overhead and sensing latency, limiting practicality for agile sensing in 6G multi-path sensing applications.
AI.method: 1) Optimize movable plate's 3D orientation once to maximize path visibility while preserving path discriminability using Fisher information analysis. 2) Perform only two predetermined linear MA scans on tilted plate to estimate elevation and azimuth AoAs. 3) Develop MAP-based AoA estimation algorithm incorporating prior AoA statistics.
AI.result: The framework enables agile multi-path sensing with significantly reduced control overhead and latency (only one orientation control and two linear scans), while achieving AoA estimation accuracy approaching that of the single-path benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed prior-guided agile multi-path sensing framework addresses practical limitations of existing MA-based methods, making multi-path sensing more feasible for 6G applications through reduced complexity and maintained accuracy.

title: Rain Rate Estimation Bounds and Weather-Adaptive Pilot Allocation for LEO Satellite ISAC
summary: Rain attenuates Ku-band satellite signals by up to 20~dB, encoding precipitation information along the Earth-space slant path. This paper derives the Bayesian Cramér-Rao bound (BCRB) for rain rate estimation from LEO broadband OFDM downlinks. Using corrected ITU-R P.838-3 coefficients, the standard CRB yields a minimum detectable rain rate $R_{\min} \approx 4.3\mmh$ for a single link at the $38^\circ$ reference elevation. We derive the prior Fisher information in closed form for log-normal rain ($c_v = 1.05$, from 186{,}292 samples) and show that a single-snapshot BCRB reduces $R_{\min}$ to $1.1\mmh$; exploiting temporal correlation ($ρ= 0.95$) over a 30-min window further tightens it to $0.95\mmh$, while multi-link fusion across $N = 215$ links lowers the operating-point RMSE \emph{lower bound} at $R = 20\mmh$ to approximately $0.07\mmh$. Building on these bounds, we formulate a weather-adaptive pilot allocation that minimizes the BCRB subject to a hard spectral-efficiency constraint, characterize its three-regime structure (full-sensing, throughput-tracking, outage), and pair it with a CUSUM rain onset detector achieving sub-10-min delay for $R \geq 20\mmh$. A closed-form analysis of dynamic LEO slant geometry identifies a sensing-optimal elevation at the P.618-validity floor of $15^\circ$ that yields a $1.58\times$ geometric improvement over the $38^\circ$ baseline, exposing a structural anti-correlation between sensing- and communication-optimal elevations along an orbital pass. Validation against 9.4~million radar samples from 215 Ku-band GEO satellite links ($r = 0.72$, RMSE~$= 1.24\dB$) and 113 rain gauges confirms the underlying attenuation model; the bounds transfer to LEO constellations under matched OFDM signal parameters, with dedicated LEO validation left for future work.

AI.tldr: Derives Bayesian Cramér-Rao bounds for rain rate estimation from LEO satellite signals, showing significant improvement with prior knowledge, temporal correlation, and multi-link fusion.
AI.motivation: Rain attenuation in Ku-band satellite signals contains precipitation information, but existing methods lack theoretical performance bounds for rain rate estimation from LEO broadband OFDM downlinks.
AI.method: Derives Bayesian Cramér-Rao bound (BCRB) for rain rate estimation, incorporates log-normal rain prior, temporal correlation, and multi-link fusion. Develops weather-adaptive pilot allocation and CUSUM rain onset detector.
AI.result: BCRB reduces minimum detectable rain rate from 4.3 mm/h to 1.1 mm/h (single snapshot), 0.95 mm/h with temporal correlation. Multi-link fusion lowers RMSE lower bound to ~0.07 mm/h at 20 mm/h. Sensing-optimal elevation is 15° (1.58× improvement over 38° baseline).
AI.conclusion: Theoretical bounds enable optimal rain sensing from LEO satellites, revealing trade-off between sensing and communication optimal elevations. Validation supports attenuation model, with bounds transferable to LEO constellations.

title: Identity-Aware U-Net: Fine-grained Cell Segmentation via Identity-Aware Representation Learning
summary: Precise segmentation of objects with highly similar shapes remains a challenging problem in dense prediction, especially in scenarios with ambiguous boundaries, overlapping instances, and weak inter-instance visual differences. While conventional segmentation models are effective at localizing object regions, they often lack the discriminative capacity required to reliably distinguish a target object from morphologically similar distractors. In this work, we study fine-grained object segmentation from an identity-aware perspective and propose Identity-Aware U-Net (IAU-Net), a unified framework that jointly models spatial localization and instance discrimination. Built upon a U-Net-style encoder-decoder architecture, our method augments the segmentation backbone with an auxiliary embedding branch that learns discriminative identity representations from high-level features, while the main branch predicts pixel-accurate masks. To enhance robustness in distinguishing objects with near-identical contours or textures, we further incorporate triplet-based metric learning, which pulls target-consistent embeddings together and separates them from hard negatives with similar morphology. This design enables the model to move beyond category-level segmentation and acquire a stronger capability for precise discrimination among visually similar objects. Experiments on benchmarks including cell segmentation demonstrate promising results, particularly in challenging cases involving similar contours, dense layouts, and ambiguous boundaries.

AI.tldr: IAU-Net is an identity-aware segmentation framework that combines spatial localization with instance discrimination using triplet metric learning to distinguish objects with highly similar shapes and ambiguous boundaries.
AI.motivation: Conventional segmentation models struggle to distinguish objects with highly similar shapes, ambiguous boundaries, overlapping instances, and weak visual differences. They lack discriminative capacity to reliably separate target objects from morphologically similar distractors.
AI.method: Proposes Identity-Aware U-Net (IAU-Net) with U-Net encoder-decoder architecture augmented by an auxiliary embedding branch that learns discriminative identity representations from high-level features. Incorporates triplet-based metric learning to pull target-consistent embeddings together and separate them from hard negatives with similar morphology.
AI.result: Experiments on benchmarks including cell segmentation show promising results, particularly in challenging cases involving similar contours, dense layouts, and ambiguous boundaries.
AI.conclusion: IAU-Net enables models to move beyond category-level segmentation and acquire stronger capability for precise discrimination among visually similar objects by jointly modeling spatial localization and instance discrimination.

title: Last-Iterate Convergence of Randomized Kaczmarz and SGD with Greedy Step Size
summary: We study last-iterate convergence of SGD with greedy step size over smooth quadratics in the interpolation regime, a setting which captures the classical Randomized Kaczmarz algorithm as well as other popular iterative linear system solvers. For these methods, we show that the $t$-th iterate attains an $O(1/t^{3/4})$ convergence rate, addressing a question posed by Attia, Schliserman, Sherman, and Koren, who gave an $O(1/t^{1/2})$ guarantee for this setting. In the proof, we introduce the family of stochastic contraction processes, whose behavior can be described by the evolution of a certain deterministic eigenvalue equation, which we analyze via a careful discrete-to-continuous reduction.

AI.tldr: SGD with greedy step size achieves O(1/t^{3/4}) last-iterate convergence for smooth quadratics in interpolation regime, improving previous O(1/t^{1/2}) bound.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses a question posed by Attia et al. about improving convergence guarantees for SGD with greedy step size in the interpolation regime, which includes important algorithms like Randomized Kaczmarz and iterative linear system solvers.
AI.method: Introduces stochastic contraction processes and analyzes their behavior through the evolution of a deterministic eigenvalue equation, using a careful discrete-to-continuous reduction technique.
AI.result: Proves that the t-th iterate attains O(1/t^{3/4}) convergence rate, improving upon the previous O(1/t^{1/2}) guarantee for SGD with greedy step size over smooth quadratics in interpolation regime.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides improved convergence analysis for SGD with greedy step size, establishing O(1/t^{3/4}) last-iterate convergence for smooth quadratics in interpolation regime, which covers important practical algorithms.

title: Hubble: An LLM-Driven Agentic Framework for Safe and Automated Alpha Factor Discovery
summary: Discovering predictive alpha factors in quantitative finance remains a formidable challenge due to the vast combinatorial search space and inherently low signal-to-noise ratios in financial data. Existing automated methods, particularly genetic programming, often produce complex, uninterpretable formulas prone to overfitting. We introduce Hubble, a closed-loop factor mining framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) as intelligent search heuristics, constrained by a domain-specific operator language and an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)-based execution sandbox. The framework evaluates candidate factors through a rigorous statistical pipeline encompassing cross-sectional Rank Information Coefficient (RankIC), annualized Information Ratio, and portfolio turnover. An evolutionary feedback mechanism returns top-performing factors and structured error diagnostics to the LLM, enabling iterative refinement across multiple generation rounds. In experiments conducted on a panel of 30 U.S. equities over 752 trading days, the system evaluated 181 syntactically valid factors from 122 unique candidates across three rounds, achieving a peak composite score of 0.827 with 100% computational stability. Our results demonstrate that combining LLM-driven generation with deterministic safety constraints yields an effective, interpretable, and reproducible approach to automated factor discovery.

AI.tldr: Hubble is a closed-loop factor mining framework using LLMs as intelligent search heuristics for discovering predictive alpha factors in quantitative finance, achieving high performance with computational stability.
AI.motivation: Discovering predictive alpha factors is challenging due to vast combinatorial search space and low signal-to-noise ratios. Existing methods like genetic programming produce complex, uninterpretable formulas prone to overfitting.
AI.method: Hubble uses LLMs as intelligent search heuristics constrained by domain-specific operator language and AST-based execution sandbox. It evaluates candidates through statistical pipeline (RankIC, Information Ratio, turnover) and uses evolutionary feedback with error diagnostics for iterative refinement.
AI.result: On 30 U.S. equities over 752 trading days, the system evaluated 181 syntactically valid factors from 122 unique candidates across three rounds, achieving peak composite score of 0.827 with 100% computational stability.
AI.conclusion: Combining LLM-driven generation with deterministic safety constraints yields an effective, interpretable, and reproducible approach to automated factor discovery in quantitative finance.

title: MonoEM-GS: Monocular Expectation-Maximization Gaussian Splatting SLAM
summary: Feed-forward geometric foundation models can infer dense point clouds and camera motion directly from RGB streams, providing priors for monocular SLAM. However, their predictions are often view-dependent and noisy: geometry can vary across viewpoints and under image transformations, and local metric properties may drift between frames. We present MonoEM-GS, a monocular mapping pipeline that integrates such geometric predictions into a global Gaussian Splatting representation while explicitly addressing these inconsistencies. MonoEM-GS couples Gaussian Splatting with an Expectation--Maximization formulation to stabilize geometry, and employs ICP-based alignment for monocular pose estimation. Beyond geometry, MonoEM-GS parameterizes Gaussians with multi-modal features, enabling in-place open-set segmentation and other downstream queries directly on the reconstructed map. We evaluate MonoEM-GS on 7-Scenes, TUM RGB-D and Replica, and compare against recent baselines.

AI.tldr: MonoEM-GS integrates noisy, view-dependent geometric predictions from foundation models into a global Gaussian Splatting representation using EM stabilization and ICP alignment for monocular SLAM.
AI.motivation: Feed-forward geometric foundation models provide priors for monocular SLAM but produce view-dependent, noisy predictions with inconsistent geometry across viewpoints and drifting metric properties.
AI.method: Couples Gaussian Splatting with Expectation-Maximization to stabilize geometry, uses ICP-based alignment for monocular pose estimation, and parameterizes Gaussians with multi-modal features for downstream tasks.
AI.result: Evaluated on 7-Scenes, TUM RGB-D and Replica datasets, showing competitive performance against recent baselines in monocular mapping.
AI.conclusion: MonoEM-GS successfully integrates geometric foundation model predictions into a consistent global representation while enabling open-set segmentation and other queries directly on the reconstructed map.

title: Generalized Roth--Lempel Codes: NMDS Characterization, Hermitian Self-Orthogonality, and Quantum Constructions
summary:
In their seminal 1989 work (IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory 35(3):655-657), Roth and Lempel constructed a well-known family of non-Reed-Solomon maximum distance separable (MDS) codes. For decades, this family of codes has attracted extensive research attention due to its algebraic structure, low-complexity decoding, and broad applications in cryptography and data storage. Most recently, in 2025, the generalized Roth-Lempel (GRL) framework unifies Roth-Lempel codes and its extensions under a flexible algebraic structure. However, explicit criteria for the near-MDS (NMDS) property of GRL codes have not been established, and no systematic construction of Hermitian self-orthogonal GRL codes has been reported, limiting their deployment in classical and quantum error correction.
  In this work, we make three contributions to address these gaps. First, we give explicit necessary and sufficient conditions for the NMDS property of the two most widely used subclasses of GRL codes. Second, we construct four new families of Hermitian self-orthogonal codes from GRL codes. Two of these families are NMDS, with parameters not covered by existing Hermitian self-orthogonal NMDS codes. Third, based on the proposed Hermitian self-orthogonal GRL codes, we construct four families of quantum GRL codes, including two infinite families of quantum NMDS codes that attain the quantum Singleton bound minus one. Compared to the known quantum error-correcting codes, we obtain many new or improved quantum error-correcting codes. This work bridges the gap between classical GRL code families and quantum error-correction applications.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses gaps in generalized Roth-Lempel (GRL) codes by establishing NMDS criteria, constructing Hermitian self-orthogonal codes, and building quantum GRL codes with improved parameters.
AI.motivation: GRL codes unify Roth-Lempel codes but lack explicit NMDS property criteria and systematic Hermitian self-orthogonal constructions, limiting their use in classical and quantum error correction.
AI.method: 1) Derive necessary and sufficient conditions for NMDS property in two main GRL subclasses. 2) Construct four families of Hermitian self-orthogonal GRL codes (two NMDS). 3) Build four families of quantum GRL codes from these constructions.
AI.result: Established NMDS criteria for GRL codes, created new Hermitian self-orthogonal NMDS codes with novel parameters, and constructed quantum NMDS codes achieving quantum Singleton bound minus one, yielding many new/improved quantum error-correcting codes.
AI.conclusion: This work bridges classical GRL codes with quantum applications by solving fundamental algebraic problems, enabling deployment of these codes in both classical and quantum error correction systems.

title: FAS-aided Robust Anti-Jamming Communications: Continuous and Discrete Positioning Designs
summary: This paper investigates the joint optimization of beamforming and antenna positions in fluid antenna system (FAS)-aided anti-jamming communications. We consider a multi-user multiple-input multiple-output downlink scenario where multiple malicious jammers exist and the jammer channel state information is imperfect. The goal is to maximize the worst-case sum-rate under quality-of-service and transmit power constraints. To achieve this, we develop two distinct optimization frameworks for continuous and discrete antenna position designs, respectively. For continuous design, we propose an alternating optimization (AO) framework that integrates successive convex approximation and majorization minimization (MM) to handle the highly non-convex problem. For discrete design, based on the minimum mean squared error criterion and MM, we reformulate the problem as a sparse recovery task and propose a low-complexity block coordinate descent and simultaneous orthogonal matching pursuit, which enables joint design rather than AO. Through systematic comparison, we uncover a practical phenomenon: the discrete joint design yields superior sum-rate performance compared to the AO-based continuous counterpart under identical conditions. This superiority stems from the sparse recovery formulation which effectively circumvents the severe local optima. Our findings challenge the conventional view that continuous optimization is inherently superior, and reveal that discretization combined with sparse recovery can offer a more effective paradigm for exploiting spatial degrees-of-freedom in FAS-aided anti-jamming communications.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes joint optimization of beamforming and antenna positions in fluid antenna systems for anti-jamming communications, showing that discrete joint design outperforms continuous optimization.
AI.motivation: Address anti-jamming communications in multi-user MIMO downlink with imperfect jammer channel information, aiming to maximize worst-case sum-rate under QoS and power constraints in fluid antenna systems.
AI.method: Two frameworks: 1) Continuous design using alternating optimization with successive convex approximation and majorization minimization; 2) Discrete design using sparse recovery formulation with block coordinate descent and simultaneous orthogonal matching pursuit for joint optimization.
AI.result: Discrete joint design yields superior sum-rate performance compared to AO-based continuous optimization under identical conditions, challenging conventional views about continuous optimization superiority.
AI.conclusion: Discretization combined with sparse recovery offers a more effective paradigm for exploiting spatial degrees-of-freedom in FAS-aided anti-jamming communications, providing better performance than continuous optimization approaches.

title: Multi-Granularity Reasoning for Image Quality Assessment via Attribute-Aware Reinforcement Learning to Rank
summary: Recent advances in reasoning-induced image quality assessment (IQA) have demonstrated the power of reinforcement learning to rank (RL2R) for training vision-language models (VLMs) to assess perceptual quality. However, existing approaches operate at a single granularity, predicting only an overall quality score, while overlooking the multi-dimensional nature of human quality perception, which encompasses attributes such as sharpness, color fidelity, noise level, and compositional aesthetics. In this paper, we propose MG-IQA (Multi-Granularity IQA), a multi-granularity reasoning framework that extends RL2R to jointly assess overall image quality and fine-grained quality attributes within a single inference pass. Our approach introduces three key innovations: (1) an attribute-aware prompting strategy that elicits structured multi-attribute reasoning from VLMs; (2) a multi-dimensional Thurstone reward model that computes attribute-specific fidelity rewards for group relative policy optimization; and (3) a cross-domain alignment mechanism that enables stable joint training across synthetic distortion, authentic distortion, and AI-generated image datasets without perceptual scale re-alignment. Extensive experiments on eight IQA benchmarks demonstrate that MG-IQA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both overall quality prediction (average SRCC improvement of 2.1\%) and attribute-level assessment, while generating interpretable, human-aligned quality descriptions.

AI.tldr: MG-IQA is a multi-granularity image quality assessment framework that uses reinforcement learning to jointly predict overall quality and fine-grained attributes in a single inference pass.
AI.motivation: Existing IQA methods only predict overall quality scores, overlooking the multi-dimensional nature of human perception which includes attributes like sharpness, color fidelity, noise level, and compositional aesthetics.
AI.method: Three key innovations: (1) attribute-aware prompting for structured multi-attribute reasoning from VLMs, (2) multi-dimensional Thurstone reward model for attribute-specific fidelity rewards, and (3) cross-domain alignment for stable joint training across different image datasets without perceptual scale re-alignment.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on eight IQA benchmarks with average SRCC improvement of 2.1% for overall quality prediction, while also excelling at attribute-level assessment and generating interpretable quality descriptions.
AI.conclusion: MG-IQA successfully extends RL2R to multi-granularity reasoning, enabling comprehensive image quality assessment that captures both overall quality and fine-grained attributes in a human-aligned manner.

title: Regularized Entropy Information Adaptation with Temporal-Awareness Networks for Simultaneous Speech Translation
summary: Simultaneous Speech Translation (SimulST) requires balancing high translation quality with low latency. Recent work introduced REINA, a method that trains a Read/Write policy based on estimating the information gain of reading more audio. However, we find that information-based policies often lack temporal context, leading the policy to bias itself toward reading most of the audio before starting to write. We improve REINA using two distinct strategies: a supervised alignment network (REINA-SAN) and a timestep-augmented network (REINA-TAN). Our results demonstrate that while both methods significantly outperform the baseline and resolve stability issues, REINA-TAN provides a slightly superior Pareto frontier for streaming efficiency, whereas REINA-SAN offers more robustness against 'read loops'. Applied to Whisper, both methods improve the pareto frontier of streaming efficiency as measured by Normalized Streaming Efficiency (NoSE) scores up to 7.1% over existing competitive baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper improves REINA for simultaneous speech translation by adding temporal context to address bias toward reading too much audio before writing, using two methods: REINA-SAN (supervised alignment) and REINA-TAN (timestep-augmented).
AI.motivation: Information-based policies like REINA lack temporal context, causing them to bias toward reading most audio before starting to write, which hurts the latency-quality tradeoff in simultaneous speech translation.
AI.method: Two strategies: 1) REINA-SAN uses a supervised alignment network, and 2) REINA-TAN uses a timestep-augmented network to provide temporal context to the read/write policy.
AI.result: Both methods significantly outperform baseline REINA and resolve stability issues. REINA-TAN provides slightly better Pareto frontier for streaming efficiency, while REINA-SAN offers more robustness against 'read loops'. Applied to Whisper, both improve streaming efficiency by up to 7.1% over competitive baselines.
AI.conclusion: Adding temporal context to information-based policies improves simultaneous speech translation by better balancing latency and quality, with REINA-TAN offering better streaming efficiency and REINA-SAN providing more robustness.

title: From Scalars to Tensors: Declared Losses Recover Epistemic Distinctions That Neutrosophic Scalars Cannot Express
summary: Leyva-Vázquez and Smarandache (2025) demonstrated that neutrosophic T/I/F evaluation, where Truth, Indeterminacy, and Falsity are independent dimensions not constrained to sum to 1.0, which reveals "hyper-truth"' (T+I+F > 1.0) in 35% of complex epistemic cases evaluated by LLMs. We extend their work in two directions. First, we replicate and extend their experiment across five model families from five vendors (Anthropic, Meta, DeepSeek, Alibaba, Mistral), finding hyper-truth in 84% of unconstrained evaluations, which confirms the phenomenon is cross-vendor under our prompt protocol. Second, and more significantly, we identify a limitation of scalar T/I/F that their framework cannot address: models adopting an `"Absorption" position (T=0, I=1, F=0) produce identical scalar outputs for fundamentally different epistemic situations (paradox, ignorance, contingency), collapsing the very distinctions neutrosophic logic was designed to preserve. We demonstrate that extending the evaluation to include declared losses (structured descriptions of what the model cannot evaluate and why) substantially recovers these distinctions. Models producing identical scalars for paradox and ignorance produce nearly disjoint loss vocabularies (Jaccard similarity < 0.10 on loss description keywords), with domain-specific, severity-rated loss declarations that differentiate the nature of their uncertainty. This suggests that scalar T/I/F is a necessary but insufficient representation of epistemic state, and that tensor-structured output (scalars + losses) provides a more faithful model of LLM epistemic capabilities.

AI.tldr: This paper extends neutrosophic T/I/F evaluation of LLMs, finding hyper-truth in 84% of cases across multiple vendors, but identifies a limitation where identical scalar outputs mask different epistemic situations, solved by adding structured loss declarations.
AI.motivation: To extend Leyva-Vázquez and Smarandache's (2025) work on neutrosophic T/I/F evaluation of LLMs by testing across more model families and addressing the limitation where identical scalar outputs (T=0, I=1, F=0) fail to distinguish between different epistemic situations like paradox, ignorance, and contingency.
AI.method: Two-part approach: 1) Replicate and extend the original experiment across five model families from five different vendors using their prompt protocol; 2) Introduce structured loss declarations (descriptions of what models cannot evaluate and why) to differentiate between epistemic situations that produce identical scalar T/I/F outputs.
AI.result: Found hyper-truth (T+I+F > 1.0) in 84% of unconstrained evaluations across all vendors, confirming the phenomenon is cross-vendor. More importantly, models producing identical scalar outputs for different epistemic situations (paradox vs ignorance) generated nearly disjoint loss vocabularies (Jaccard similarity < 0.10), with domain-specific, severity-rated loss declarations that successfully differentiated the nature of their uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Scalar T/I/F evaluation is necessary but insufficient for representing LLM epistemic states. Tensor-structured output combining scalars with structured loss declarations provides a more faithful model of LLM epistemic capabilities by preserving distinctions that neutrosophic logic was designed to capture.

title: AWARE: Adaptive Whole-body Active Rotating Control for Enhanced LiDAR-Inertial Odometry under Human-in-the-Loop Interaction
summary: Human-in-the-loop (HITL) UAV operation is essential in complex and safety-critical aerial surveying environments, where human operators provide navigation intent while onboard autonomy must maintain accurate and robust state estimation. A key challenge in this setting is that resource-constrained UAV platforms are often limited to narrow-field-of-view LiDAR sensors. In geometrically degenerate or feature-sparse scenes, limited sensing coverage often weakens LiDAR Inertial Odometry (LIO)'s observability, causing drift accumulation, degraded geometric accuracy, and unstable state estimation, which directly compromise safe and effective HITL operation and the reliability of downstream surveying products. To overcome this limitation, we present AWARE, a bio-inspired whole-body active yawing framework that exploits the UAV's own rotational agility to extend the effective sensor horizon and improve LIO's observability without additional mechanical actuation. The core of AWARE is a differentiable Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework embedded in a Reinforcement Learning (RL) loop. It first identifies the viewing direction that maximizes information gain across the full yaw space, and a lightweight RL agent then adjusts the MPC cost weights online according to the current environmental context, enabling an adaptive balance between estimation accuracy and flight stability. A Safe Flight Corridor mechanism further ensures operational safety within this HITL paradigm by decoupling the operator's navigational intent from autonomous yaw optimization to enable safe and efficient cooperative control. We validate AWARE through extensive experiments in diverse simulated and real-world environments.

AI.tldr: AWARE: Bio-inspired active yawing framework for UAVs that improves LiDAR odometry observability in feature-sparse environments by optimizing viewing direction using MPC+RL, enabling safer human-in-the-loop operation.
AI.motivation: Narrow-FOV LiDAR sensors on resource-constrained UAVs struggle in geometrically degenerate or feature-sparse scenes, causing drift and unstable state estimation that compromises safe human-in-the-loop operation and surveying reliability.
AI.method: AWARE uses a differentiable MPC framework embedded in RL loop to identify optimal viewing directions maximizing information gain. RL agent adaptively adjusts MPC cost weights based on environmental context. Safe Flight Corridor decouples operator navigation intent from autonomous yaw optimization.
AI.result: Validated through extensive experiments in diverse simulated and real-world environments, showing improved LIO observability without additional mechanical actuation.
AI.conclusion: AWARE enables UAVs to extend effective sensor horizon through rotational agility, improving state estimation accuracy while maintaining flight stability and operational safety in human-in-the-loop surveying scenarios.

title: On Demand-Private Coded Caching With Multiple Demands
summary: We consider a coded caching problem with multiple demands under a privacy constraint. In this problem, a server with access to \(N\) files serves \(K\) users over a shared link, and each user requests \(L\) distinct files. The privacy constraint requires that each user obtain no information about the demands of the other users. We propose a new achievable scheme for arbitrary numbers of files and users. The scheme is obtained via a transformation from a non-private coded caching scheme under uncoded placement for \(N\) files and \(K \cdot \min\{N,KL\}\) users, where each user requests one file and the demands are restricted to a subset of all possible demands. We then derive a converse bound, and the proposed scheme is shown to be order optimal within a factor of 6 of this bound.

AI.tldr: A coded caching scheme with privacy constraints for multiple demands per user, achieving order optimality within factor 6.
AI.motivation: Privacy concerns in coded caching systems where users request multiple files and should not learn about other users' demands.
AI.method: Transform non-private coded caching scheme (with uncoded placement) for N files and K·min{N,KL} users to create private scheme; restrict demands to subset.
AI.result: Proposed scheme is order optimal within factor 6 of derived converse bound.
AI.conclusion: Privacy in coded caching with multiple demands can be achieved with order-optimal performance using transformation approach.

title: Unsupervised Equivalent Contrastive Learning for Radio Signal Recognition
summary: Robust radio signal recognition is fundamental to spectrum management, electromagnetic space security, and intelligent wireless applications, yet existing deep-learning methods rely heavily on large labeled datasets and struggle to capture the multi-domain characteristics inherent in real-world signals. To address these limitations, we propose an unsupervised equivalent contrastive learning method that leverages four information-lossless equivalent transformations, spanning the time, instantaneous, frequency, and time-frequency domains, to construct multi-view and semantically consistent representations of each signal. An equivalent contrastive learning strategy then aligns these complementary views to learn discriminative and transferable embeddings without requiring labeled data. Once pre-training is completed, the resulting model can be directly fine-tuned on downstream tasks using only raw signal samples, without reapplying any equivalent transformations, which reduces computational overhead and simplifies deployment. Extensive experiments on four public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art contrastive baselines under linear evaluation, few-shot semi-supervised learning, and cross-domain transfer settings. Notably, the learned representations yield substantial gains in few-shot regimes and challenging channel conditions, confirming the effectiveness of multi-domain equivalent modeling in enhancing robustness and generalization. This work establishes a principled pathway for exploiting massive unlabeled radio data and provides a foundation for future self-supervised learning frameworks in wireless systems.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised equivalent contrastive learning method for radio signal recognition using multi-domain transformations to learn robust representations without labeled data.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods for radio signal recognition require large labeled datasets and fail to capture multi-domain characteristics of real-world signals, limiting robustness and generalization.
AI.method: Proposes unsupervised equivalent contrastive learning with four information-lossless transformations (time, instantaneous, frequency, time-frequency domains) to create multi-view representations, then aligns these views to learn discriminative embeddings without labels.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art contrastive baselines on four public datasets in linear evaluation, few-shot semi-supervised learning, and cross-domain transfer settings, with substantial gains in few-shot regimes and challenging channel conditions.
AI.conclusion: The method establishes a principled pathway for exploiting unlabeled radio data, enhances robustness through multi-domain modeling, and provides foundation for future self-supervised learning in wireless systems.

title: The Deployment Gap in AI Media Detection: Platform-Aware and Visually Constrained Adversarial Evaluation
summary:
Recent AI media detectors report near-perfect performance under clean laboratory evaluation, yet their robustness under realistic deployment conditions remains underexplored. In practice, AI-generated images are resized, compressed, re-encoded, and visually modified before being shared on online platforms. We argue that this creates a deployment gap between laboratory robustness and real-world reliability.
  In this work, we introduce a platform-aware adversarial evaluation framework for AI media detection that explicitly models deployment transforms (e.g., resizing, compression, screenshot-style distortions) and constrains perturbations to visually plausible meme-style bands rather than full-image noise. Under this threat model, detectors achieving AUC $\approx$ 0{.}99 in clean settings experience substantial degradation. Per-image platform-aware attacks reduce AUC to significantly lower levels and achieve high fake-to-real misclassification rates, despite strict visual constraints. We further demonstrate that universal perturbations exist even under localized band constraints, revealing shared vulnerability directions across inputs. Beyond accuracy degradation, we observe pronounced calibration collapse under attack, where detectors become confidently incorrect.
  Our findings highlight that robustness measured under clean conditions substantially overestimates deployment robustness. We advocate for platform-aware evaluation as a necessary component of future AI media security benchmarks and release our evaluation framework to facilitate standardized robustness assessment.

AI.tldr: AI media detectors perform well in lab tests but fail in real-world conditions due to platform transformations like resizing, compression, and visual modifications. A new evaluation framework reveals severe vulnerabilities when detectors face realistic deployment scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current AI media detectors show near-perfect performance in clean lab evaluations but their robustness in real-world deployment is unknown. In practice, AI-generated images undergo various platform transformations (resizing, compression, re-encoding, visual modifications) before being shared online, creating a gap between lab performance and real-world reliability.
AI.method: The paper introduces a platform-aware adversarial evaluation framework that models deployment transforms (resizing, compression, screenshot-style distortions) and constrains perturbations to visually plausible meme-style bands rather than full-image noise. This includes per-image attacks and universal perturbations under localized band constraints.
AI.result: Detectors achieving AUC ≈ 0.99 in clean settings experience substantial degradation under platform-aware attacks. Per-image attacks significantly reduce AUC and achieve high fake-to-real misclassification rates. Universal perturbations exist even under localized constraints, revealing shared vulnerabilities. Detectors also suffer from calibration collapse, becoming confidently incorrect under attack.
AI.conclusion: Robustness measured under clean conditions substantially overestimates deployment robustness. Platform-aware evaluation is necessary for future AI media security benchmarks. The authors release their evaluation framework to facilitate standardized robustness assessment.

title: A Tale of Two Temperatures: Simple, Efficient, and Diverse Sampling from Diffusion Language Models
summary: Much work has been done on designing fast and accurate sampling for diffusion language models (dLLMs). However, these efforts have largely focused on the tradeoff between speed and quality of individual samples; how to additionally ensure diversity across samples remains less well understood. In this work, we show that diversity can be increased by using softened, tempered versions of familiar confidence-based remasking heuristics, retaining their computational benefits and offering simple implementations. We motivate this approach by introducing an idealized formal model of fork tokens and studying the impact of remasking on the expected entropy at the forks. Empirically, the proposed tempered heuristics close the exploration gap (pass@k) between existing confidence-based and autoregressive sampling, hence outperforming both when controlling for cost (pass@NFE). We further study how the increase in diversity translates to downstream post-training and test-time compute scaling. Overall, our findings demonstrate that simple, efficient, and diverse sampling from dLLMs is possible.

AI.tldr: Tempered confidence-based remasking heuristics improve diversity in diffusion language model sampling while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing sampling methods for diffusion language models focus on speed-quality tradeoffs but neglect diversity across samples, creating an exploration gap compared to autoregressive sampling.
AI.method: Proposes softened, tempered versions of confidence-based remasking heuristics, motivated by formal analysis of fork tokens and their impact on expected entropy. The approach retains computational benefits with simple implementations.
AI.result: Tempered heuristics close the exploration gap (pass@k) between existing confidence-based and autoregressive sampling, outperforming both when controlling for cost (pass@NFE). Also shows improved diversity translates to better downstream post-training and test-time compute scaling.
AI.conclusion: Simple, efficient, and diverse sampling from diffusion language models is achievable through tempered confidence-based remasking heuristics.

title: LLMs for Text-Based Exploration and Navigation Under Partial Observability
comment: 15 pages, (to be published Springer Lecture Notes of the Institute for Computer Sciences, Social Informatics and Telecommunications Engineering [LNICST] )
summary: Exploration and goal-directed navigation in unknown layouts are central to inspection, logistics, and search-and-rescue. We ask whether large language models (LLMs) can function as \emph{text-only} controllers under partial observability -- without code execution, tools, or program synthesis. We introduce a reproducible benchmark with oracle localisation in fixed ASCII gridworlds: each step reveals only a local $5\times5$ window around the agent and the model must select one of \texttt{UP/RIGHT/DOWN/LEFT}. Nine contemporary LLMs ranging from open/proprietary, dense / Mixture of Experts and instruction- vs. reasoning-tuned are evaluated on two tasks across three layouts of increasing difficulty: \emph{Exploration} (maximising revealed cells) and \emph{Navigation} (reach the goal on the shortest path). The experimental results are evaluated on quantitative metrics including \emph{success rate}, \emph{efficiency} such as normalised coverage and \emph{path length} vs. oracle as well as qualitative analysis. Reasoning-tuned models reliably complete navigation across all layouts, yet remain less efficient than oracle paths. Few-shot demonstrations in the prompt chiefly help these Reasoning-tuned models by reducing invalid moves and shortening paths, while classic dense instruction models remain inconsistent. We observe characteristic action priors (UP/RIGHT) that can induce looping under partial observability. Overall, training regimen and test-time deliberation predict control ability better than raw parameter count. These findings suggest lightweight hybridisation with classical online planners as a practical route to deployable partial map systems.

AI.tldr: LLMs can function as text-only controllers for exploration and navigation in partially observable gridworlds, with reasoning-tuned models performing best but less efficiently than optimal paths.
AI.motivation: To determine if LLMs can serve as text-only controllers for exploration and goal-directed navigation in unknown environments under partial observability, without requiring code execution or program synthesis.
AI.method: Created a reproducible benchmark with oracle localization in fixed ASCII gridworlds where each step reveals only a local 5x5 window. Evaluated 9 contemporary LLMs (open/proprietary, dense/Mixture of Experts, instruction/reasoning-tuned) on exploration (maximizing revealed cells) and navigation (shortest path to goal) tasks across three layouts of increasing difficulty.
AI.result: Reasoning-tuned models reliably complete navigation across all layouts but remain less efficient than oracle paths. Few-shot demonstrations help reasoning-tuned models by reducing invalid moves and shortening paths, while instruction models remain inconsistent. Models show characteristic action priors (UP/RIGHT) that can cause looping under partial observability.
AI.conclusion: Training regimen and test-time deliberation predict control ability better than raw parameter count. Findings suggest lightweight hybridization with classical online planners as a practical route to deployable partial map systems.

title: OmniUMI: Towards Physically Grounded Robot Learning via Human-Aligned Multimodal Interaction
summary: UMI-style interfaces enable scalable robot learning, but existing systems remain largely visuomotor, relying primarily on RGB observations and trajectory while providing only limited access to physical interaction signals. This becomes a fundamental limitation in contact-rich manipulation, where success depends on contact dynamics such as tactile interaction, internal grasping force, and external interaction wrench that are difficult to infer from vision alone. We present OmniUMI, a unified framework for physically grounded robot learning via human-aligned multimodal interaction. OmniUMI synchronously captures RGB, depth, trajectory, tactile sensing, internal grasping force, and external interaction wrench within a compact handheld system, while maintaining collection--deployment consistency through a shared embodiment design. To support human-aligned demonstration, OmniUMI provides dual-force feedback through bilateral gripper feedback and natural perception of external interaction wrench in the handheld embodiment. Built on this interface, we extend diffusion policy with visual, tactile, and force-related observations, and deploy the learned policy through impedance-based execution for unified regulation of motion and contact behavior. Experiments demonstrate reliable sensing and strong downstream performance on force-sensitive pick-and-place, interactive surface erasing, and tactile-informed selective release. Overall, OmniUMI combines physically grounded multimodal data acquisition with human-aligned interaction, providing a scalable foundation for learning contact-rich manipulation.

AI.tldr: OmniUMI is a unified framework for contact-rich robot learning that captures multimodal physical interaction signals (tactile, force, wrench) alongside vision and trajectory, enabling human-aligned demonstrations and improved manipulation policies.
AI.motivation: Existing robot learning systems are primarily visuomotor and lack access to crucial physical interaction signals needed for contact-rich manipulation tasks, where tactile sensing, internal grasping force, and external interaction wrench are essential for success.
AI.method: OmniUMI is a compact handheld system that synchronously captures RGB, depth, trajectory, tactile sensing, internal grasping force, and external interaction wrench. It features dual-force feedback for human-aligned demonstration and uses diffusion policy extended with multimodal observations, deployed through impedance-based execution.
AI.result: The system demonstrates reliable sensing and strong downstream performance on force-sensitive tasks including pick-and-place, interactive surface erasing, and tactile-informed selective release, showing improved contact-rich manipulation capabilities.
AI.conclusion: OmniUMI provides a scalable foundation for learning contact-rich manipulation by combining physically grounded multimodal data acquisition with human-aligned interaction, addressing the limitations of vision-only approaches.

title: ISAC-Enabled Non-Terrestrial Networks for 6G: Design Principles, Standardization, Performance Tradeoffs, and Use Cases
summary: Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTN) have emerged as a key enabler to fully realize the vision of integrated, intelligent, and ubiquitous connectivity in 6G systems. However, several operational challenges, including severe Doppler effects, interference, and latency, hinder the seamless integration of NTN and Terrestrial Networks (TN). In this context, Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC), which unifies sensing and communication functionalities within a common framework, offers great potential to address these challenges while enabling new network capabilities. Due to its complementary functionalities, ISAC can play a pivotal role in enhancing NTN performance, although its practical adoption requires a fundamental rethinking of existing architectural and standardization frameworks. Motivated by this need, this article examines key aspects of ISAC-enabled NTN, including architectural design principles, application scenarios, standardization challenges, and key performance tradeoffs. Finally, a representative case study is presented to illustrate major technical challenges and highlight promising future research directions for ISAC-enabled NTN.

AI.tldr: ISAC-enabled Non-Terrestrial Networks can address key NTN challenges like Doppler effects, interference, and latency while enabling new 6G capabilities, but require architectural redesign and standardization.
AI.motivation: NTN faces operational challenges (severe Doppler effects, interference, latency) that hinder seamless integration with terrestrial networks for 6G's vision of ubiquitous connectivity. ISAC offers potential to address these challenges while enabling new network capabilities.
AI.method: The article examines ISAC-enabled NTN through architectural design principles, application scenarios, standardization challenges, and key performance tradeoffs. It includes a representative case study to illustrate technical challenges and future research directions.
AI.result: ISAC can play a pivotal role in enhancing NTN performance by unifying sensing and communication functionalities, but practical adoption requires fundamental rethinking of existing architectural and standardization frameworks.
AI.conclusion: ISAC-enabled NTN presents promising solutions for 6G integration challenges, though significant architectural redesign and standardization efforts are needed to realize its full potential for future integrated networks.

title: Bayesian Cramér-Rao Bound for Sensing Performance in Meta-Backscatter Systems
summary: Meta-backscatter system that utilizes meta-material sensors is a promising enabler for future environmental sensing, offering distinct advantages such as low cost, zero-power consumption, and robustness. Specifically, the electromagnetic response of the sensor, typically characterized by a frequency-selective absorption profile, is affected by the environmental conditions, allowing the estimation of these conditions from the reflected signal. However, it remains unclear what estimation accuracy can be achieved fundamentally. Motivated by this gap, we quantify this accuracy limit using the Bayesian Cramér-Rao bound (BCRB), which provides a lower bound on the mean-squared error for the environmental condition. Establishing this limit is challenging because the electromagnetic response of the sensor is distorted by the channel fading, while the channel estimation is infeasible since the sensors cannot be configured to predefined states to generate training data. To address this challenge, we consider the joint BCRB of the channel coefficient and the environmental condition in a multicarrier framework. The BCRB of the environmental condition is then obtained by selecting the corresponding element from the joint BCRB. An analysis of the derived BCRB reveals the impact of the absorption peak shape and the number of subcarriers. The derivation and analysis of the BCRB are verified through simulations.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes fundamental accuracy limits for meta-backscatter environmental sensing using Bayesian Cramér-Rao bound analysis.
AI.motivation: Meta-backscatter systems with meta-material sensors offer low-cost, zero-power environmental sensing, but there's no clear understanding of the fundamental estimation accuracy limits for environmental conditions.
AI.method: Uses Bayesian Cramér-Rao bound (BCRB) to quantify accuracy limits, addressing challenges of channel fading distortion and infeasible channel estimation by considering joint BCRB of channel coefficient and environmental condition in multicarrier framework.
AI.result: Derived BCRB reveals impact of absorption peak shape and number of subcarriers on estimation accuracy, with verification through simulations.
AI.conclusion: The BCRB analysis provides fundamental accuracy limits for meta-backscatter environmental sensing, offering insights into system design parameters for optimal performance.

title: Orthogonal Quadratic Complements for Vision Transformer Feed-Forward Networks
summary:
Recent bilinear feed-forward replacements for vision transformers can substantially improve accuracy, but they often conflate two effects: stronger second-order interactions and increased redundancy relative to the main branch. We study a complementary design principle in which auxiliary quadratic features contribute only information not already captured by the dominant hidden representation. To this end, we propose Orthogonal Quadratic Complements (OQC), which construct a low-rank quadratic auxiliary branch and explicitly project it onto the orthogonal complement of the main branch before injection. We further study an efficient low-rank realization (OQC-LR) and gated extensions (OQC-static and OQC-dynamic).
  Under a parameter-matched Deep-ViT and CIFAR-100 protocol with a fixed penultimate residual readout, full OQC improves an AFBO baseline from 64.25 +/- 0.22 to 65.59 +/- 0.22, while OQC-LR reaches 65.52 +/- 0.25 with a substantially better speed-accuracy tradeoff. On TinyImageNet, the gated extension OQC-dynamic achieves 51.88 +/- 0.32, improving the baseline (50.45 +/- 0.21) by 1.43 points and outperforming all ungated variants. Mechanism analyses show near-zero post-projection auxiliary-main overlap together with improved representation geometry and class separation. The full family, including both ungated and gated variants, generalizes consistently across both datasets.

AI.tldr: OQC introduces orthogonal quadratic complements for vision transformers, creating auxiliary quadratic features that only add information not already in the main branch, improving accuracy with better speed-accuracy tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: Recent bilinear feed-forward replacements improve accuracy but conflate two effects: stronger second-order interactions and increased redundancy. The authors want to study a complementary design where auxiliary quadratic features contribute only new information not already captured by the main branch.
AI.method: Propose Orthogonal Quadratic Complements (OQC) which construct a low-rank quadratic auxiliary branch and explicitly project it onto the orthogonal complement of the main branch before injection. Also study efficient low-rank realization (OQC-LR) and gated extensions (OQC-static and OQC-dynamic).
AI.result: On CIFAR-100, full OQC improves AFBO baseline from 64.25 to 65.59, while OQC-LR reaches 65.52 with better speed-accuracy tradeoff. On TinyImageNet, OQC-dynamic achieves 51.88, improving baseline (50.45) by 1.43 points. Mechanism analyses show near-zero post-projection auxiliary-main overlap with improved representation geometry and class separation.
AI.conclusion: OQC effectively creates orthogonal quadratic complements that add only novel information to the main branch, improving vision transformer performance across datasets with consistent generalization in both ungated and gated variants.

title: K-STEMIT: Knowledge-Informed Spatio-Temporal Efficient Multi-Branch Graph Neural Network for Subsurface Stratigraphy Thickness Estimation from Radar Data
summary: Subsurface stratigraphy contains important spatio-temporal information about accumulation, deformation, and layer formation in polar ice sheets. In particular, variations in internal ice layer thickness provide valuable constraints for snow mass balance estimation and projections of ice sheet change. Although radar sensors can capture these layered structures as depth-resolved radargrams, convolutional neural networks applied directly to radar images are often sensitive to speckle noise and acquisition artifacts. In addition, purely data-driven methods may underuse physical knowledge, leading to unrealistic thickness estimates under spatial or temporal extrapolation. To address these challenges, we develop K-STEMIT, a novel knowledge-informed, efficient, multi-branch spatio-temporal graph neural network that combines a geometric framework for spatial learning with temporal convolution to capture temporal dynamics, and incorporates physical data synchronized from the Model Atmospheric Regional physical weather model. An adaptive feature fusion strategy is employed to dynamically combine features learned from different branches. Extensive experiments have been conducted to compare K-STEMIT against current state-of-the-art methods in both knowledge-informed and non-knowledge-informed settings, as well as other existing methods. Results show that K-STEMIT consistently achieves the highest accuracy while maintaining near-optimal efficiency. Most notably, incorporating adaptive feature fusion and physical priors reduces the root mean-squared error by 21.01% with negligible additional cost compared to its conventional multi-branch variants. Additionally, our proposed K-STEMIT achieves consistently lower per-year relative MAE, enabling reliable, continuous spatiotemporal assessment of snow accumulation variability across large spatial regions.

AI.tldr: K-STEMIT: A knowledge-informed spatio-temporal graph neural network that combines geometric spatial learning, temporal convolution, and physical weather model data to estimate ice layer thickness from radargrams with improved accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing CNN-based methods for analyzing subsurface ice layer thickness from radargrams are sensitive to noise and artifacts, and purely data-driven approaches underuse physical knowledge, leading to unrealistic estimates during extrapolation. There's a need for methods that can reliably capture spatio-temporal dynamics of ice layer formation while incorporating physical constraints.
AI.method: Developed K-STEMIT, a knowledge-informed multi-branch spatio-temporal graph neural network that combines: 1) geometric framework for spatial learning, 2) temporal convolution to capture temporal dynamics, 3) physical data from Model Atmospheric Regional weather model, and 4) adaptive feature fusion strategy to dynamically combine features from different branches.
AI.result: K-STEMIT consistently achieves highest accuracy while maintaining near-optimal efficiency. Adaptive feature fusion and physical priors reduce root mean-squared error by 21.01% with negligible additional cost compared to conventional multi-branch variants. Achieves consistently lower per-year relative MAE, enabling reliable continuous spatiotemporal assessment of snow accumulation variability.
AI.conclusion: K-STEMIT successfully addresses limitations of existing methods by integrating physical knowledge with deep learning, providing more accurate and reliable estimates of ice layer thickness for snow mass balance estimation and ice sheet change projections across large spatial regions.

title: Evaluating Reliability Gaps in Large Language Model Safety via Repeated Prompt Sampling
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures; accepted at the CCAI 2026 conference
summary: Traditional benchmarks for large language models (LLMs), such as HELM and AIR-BENCH, primarily assess safety risk through breadth-oriented evaluation across diverse tasks. However, real-world deployment often exposes a different class of risk: operational failures arising from repeated generations of the same prompt rather than broad task generalization. In high-stakes settings, response consistency and safety under repeated use are critical operational requirements. We introduce Accelerated Prompt Stress Testing (APST), a depth-oriented evaluation framework inspired by highly accelerated stress testing in reliability engineering. APST probes LLM behavior by repeatedly sampling identical prompts under controlled operational conditions, including temperature variation and prompt perturbation, to surface latent failure modes such as hallucinations, refusal inconsistency, and unsafe completions. Rather than treating failures as isolated events, APST characterizes them statistically as stochastic outcomes of repeated inference. We model observed safety failures using Bernoulli and binomial formulations to estimate per-inference failure probabilities, enabling quantitative comparison of operational risk across models and configurations. We apply APST to multiple instruction-tuned LLMs evaluated on AIR-BENCH 2024 derived safety and security prompts. While models exhibit similar performance under conventional single- or very-low-sample evaluation (N <= 3), repeated sampling reveals substantial variation in empirical failure probabilities across temperatures. These results demonstrate that shallow benchmark scores can obscure meaningful differences in reliability under sustained use.

AI.tldr: APST is a depth-oriented evaluation framework that stress tests LLMs by repeatedly sampling identical prompts to uncover latent failure modes that aren't visible in traditional breadth-oriented benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Traditional LLM benchmarks focus on breadth across diverse tasks but miss operational risks from repeated use of the same prompts in real-world deployment, where consistency and safety under sustained use are critical.
AI.method: APST repeatedly samples identical prompts under controlled conditions (temperature variation, prompt perturbation) to surface latent failures like hallucinations and refusal inconsistency. It models failures statistically using Bernoulli/binomial formulations to estimate per-inference failure probabilities.
AI.result: While models show similar performance under conventional single/low-sample evaluation, repeated sampling reveals substantial variation in empirical failure probabilities across temperatures, showing shallow benchmarks can obscure reliability differences.
AI.conclusion: Depth-oriented stress testing like APST is necessary to properly assess LLM operational reliability, as traditional breadth-oriented benchmarks miss critical failure modes that emerge under repeated use in real-world deployment.

title: LIDEA: Human-to-Robot Imitation Learning via Implicit Feature Distillation and Explicit Geometry Alignment
summary: Scaling up robot learning is hindered by the scarcity of robotic demonstrations, whereas human videos offer a vast, untapped source of interaction data. However, bridging the embodiment gap between human hands and robot arms remains a critical challenge. Existing cross-embodiment transfer strategies typically rely on visual editing, but they often introduce visual artifacts due to intrinsic discrepancies in visual appearance and 3D geometry. To address these limitations, we introduce LIDEA (Implicit Feature Distillation and Explicit Geometric Alignment), an imitation learning framework in which policy learning benefits from human demonstrations. In the 2D visual domain, LIDEA employs a dual-stage transitive distillation pipeline that aligns human and robot representations in a shared latent space. In the 3D geometric domain, we propose an embodiment-agnostic alignment strategy that explicitly decouples embodiment from interaction geometry, ensuring consistent 3D-aware perception. Extensive experiments empirically validate LIDEA from two perspectives: data efficiency and OOD robustness. Results show that human data substitutes up to 80% of costly robot demonstrations, and the framework successfully transfers unseen patterns from human videos for out-of-distribution generalization.

AI.tldr: LIDEA is an imitation learning framework that enables robots to learn from human videos by addressing the embodiment gap through implicit feature distillation and explicit geometric alignment.
AI.motivation: Robot learning is limited by scarce robotic demonstrations, while human videos offer abundant interaction data. However, the embodiment gap between human hands and robot arms presents a critical challenge that existing visual editing approaches struggle with due to visual artifacts from appearance and geometry discrepancies.
AI.method: LIDEA uses a dual-stage approach: (1) In 2D visual domain, transitive distillation aligns human and robot representations in shared latent space; (2) In 3D geometric domain, embodiment-agnostic alignment decouples embodiment from interaction geometry for consistent 3D-aware perception.
AI.result: Human data can substitute up to 80% of costly robot demonstrations. The framework successfully transfers unseen patterns from human videos for out-of-distribution generalization, showing improvements in both data efficiency and OOD robustness.
AI.conclusion: LIDEA effectively bridges the embodiment gap between humans and robots, enabling efficient use of abundant human video data for robot learning while maintaining robustness to distribution shifts.

title: Optimal Codes for Deterministic Identification over Gaussian Channels: Closing the Capacity Gap
summary: Deterministic identification (DI) has emerged as a promising paradigm for large-scale and goal-oriented communication systems. Despite significant progress, a fundamental open problem has remained unresolved: a persistent gap between the best known lower and upper bounds on the DI capacity, as well as on the corresponding rate-reliability tradeoff bounds. In this paper, we finally close this gap for Gaussian channels $\mathcal{G}$ by constructing an optimised code that achieves the known upper bound. This allows us to establish that the linearithmic capacity for deterministic identification is $\dot{C}_{\text{DI}}(\mathcal{G})=\frac{1}{2}$. Furthermore, we analyse the rate-reliability tradeoff and show that the proposed scheme matches the known upper bounds to first order, thereby closing the existing gap in reliability performance for all admissible error decay regimes. Finally, we demonstrate the existence of an optimum universal code, which does not require knowledge of the channel parameters and yet achieves capacity.

AI.tldr: The paper closes the gap between lower and upper bounds on deterministic identification capacity for Gaussian channels, establishing the exact capacity as 1/2 and matching rate-reliability tradeoff bounds.
AI.motivation: Deterministic identification (DI) is important for large-scale goal-oriented communication, but there has been a persistent gap between known lower and upper bounds on DI capacity and rate-reliability tradeoffs that needed to be resolved.
AI.method: Constructed an optimized code that achieves the known upper bound for Gaussian channels, analyzed the rate-reliability tradeoff, and demonstrated existence of an optimum universal code that doesn't require channel parameter knowledge.
AI.result: Established that the linearithmic capacity for deterministic identification is 1/2, closed the gap in reliability performance for all error decay regimes, and showed existence of a universal capacity-achieving code.
AI.conclusion: The paper finally resolves the fundamental open problem by closing the gap between DI capacity bounds for Gaussian channels, providing exact capacity results and optimal universal coding schemes.

title: Reliable Online Resource Allocation for Multi-User Semantic Communications: A Constraint Bayesian Optimization Approach
comment: 13 pages, 9 figures. The paper has been submitted for potential journal publications
summary: Semantic communication has been increasingly integrated into edge computing systems for reconstruction tasks, owing to its advantages in source compression, robustness to channel noise, and task execution efficiency. However, the black-box nature of neural-network (NN)-based semantic codecs, together with the noisy transmission of semantic features, makes it difficult to allocate transmission resources and guarantee reconstruction quality for multiple users. In this paper, we propose a reliable online resource allocation framework for a semantic-driven multi-user edge computing system, where multiple users encode source information into semantic features and offload reconstruction to an edge server. We formulate a multi-user resource optimization problem whose objective jointly accounts for system-wide reconstruction performance and transmission latency, under constraints that guarantee each user's minimum reconstruction quality. To solve this problem, we develop a Bayesian optimization (BO)-based online algorithm that enables flexible control of the user-side semantic compression ratio (CR) and allocation of transmission rates. The edge server jointly determines each user's CR and transmission rate by exploiting Gaussian-process (GP) models that capture the relationship between reconstruction performance, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and CR, and by employing an acquisition function to select CRs that satisfy the performance quality constraints while maximizing the objective. Simulation results on high-resolution video-frame reconstruction datasets demonstrate that the proposed method selects near-optimal CRs via the GP surrogate and acquisition function, achieving a 98.03% constraint-satisfaction rate and reducing transmission latency by more than 45% compared with fixed-CR schemes.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Bayesian optimization-based online resource allocation framework for semantic-driven multi-user edge computing systems to jointly optimize reconstruction quality and transmission latency while guaranteeing minimum quality constraints.
AI.motivation: Semantic communication in edge computing faces challenges due to the black-box nature of neural-network-based semantic codecs and noisy transmission, making it difficult to allocate transmission resources and guarantee reconstruction quality for multiple users.
AI.method: Develops a Bayesian optimization-based online algorithm using Gaussian-process models to capture relationships between reconstruction performance, SNR, and compression ratio, with an acquisition function to select compression ratios that satisfy quality constraints while maximizing the joint objective.
AI.result: Achieves 98.03% constraint-satisfaction rate, reduces transmission latency by more than 45% compared to fixed-compression-ratio schemes, and selects near-optimal compression ratios via the GP surrogate model.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively solves the multi-user resource optimization problem in semantic-driven edge computing systems, balancing reconstruction quality and transmission efficiency while guaranteeing minimum quality requirements.

title: Robust Fair Disease Diagnosis in CT Images
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at the 3rd Workshop on New Trends in AI-Generated Media and Security (AIMS) @ CVPR 2026
summary: Automated diagnosis from chest CT has improved considerably with deep learning, but models trained on skewed datasets tend to perform unevenly across patient demographics. However, the situation is worse than simple demographic bias. In clinical data, class imbalance and group underrepresentation often coincide, creating compound failure modes that neither standard rebalancing nor fairness corrections can fix alone. We introduce a two-level objective that targets both axes of this problem. Logit-adjusted cross-entropy loss operates at the sample level, shifting decision margins by class frequency with provable consistency guarantees. Conditional Value at Risk aggregation operates at the group level, directing optimization pressure toward whichever demographic group currently has the higher loss. We evaluate on the Fair Disease Diagnosis benchmark using a 3D ResNet-18 pretrained on Kinetics-400, classifying CT volumes into Adenocarcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, COVID-19, and Normal groups with patient sex annotations. The training set illustrates the compound problem concretely: squamous cell carcinoma has 84 samples total, 5 of them female. The combined loss reaches a gender-averaged macro F1 of 0.8403 with a fairness gap of 0.0239, a 13.3% improvement in score and 78% reduction in demographic disparity over the baseline. Ablations show that each component alone falls short. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/Purdue-M2/Fair-Disease-Diagnosis.

AI.tldr: Two-level objective combining logit-adjusted cross-entropy for class imbalance and Conditional Value at Risk for group fairness improves chest CT diagnosis performance and reduces demographic disparities.
AI.motivation: Automated chest CT diagnosis suffers from compound failure modes where class imbalance and demographic underrepresentation coincide, creating problems that standard rebalancing or fairness methods alone cannot fix.
AI.method: Two-level approach: (1) Logit-adjusted cross-entropy loss at sample level to handle class imbalance with provable consistency guarantees, and (2) Conditional Value at Risk aggregation at group level to direct optimization toward whichever demographic group currently has higher loss.
